summary: A Chinese dynasty that ruled from 1368 to 1644, founded by the Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang) after overthrowing the Mongol Yuan dynasty and succeeded by the Manchu Qing dynasty following the fall of Beijing to the rebel Li Zicheng. Its third ruler, the Yongle Emperor, moved the capital from Nanjing to Beijing, built the Forbidden City, and sponsored the eunuch admiral Zheng He's massive treasure-fleet voyages across the Indian Ocean. The dynasty's later history was dominated by powerful court eunuchs (the 'Eight Tigers' leader Liu Jin, then Wei Zhongxian), a humiliating Mongol capture of the Zhengtong Emperor at the Tumu Crisis, and a final collapse amid famine-driven peasant rebellion and the general Wu Sangui's betrayal at the Great Wall. Renowned for its Jingdezhen porcelain and as the era of classic novels like Journey to the West and Romance of the Three Kingdoms, it faced chronic wokou pirate raids and, after its fall, was the object of loyalist restoration efforts by figures like Koxinga.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How the dynasty is identified in giveaways: a Chinese dynasty ruling 1368-1644, founded by the Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang) after the collapse of the Mongol Yuan dynasty and eventually succeeded by the Qing dynasty following the Manchu conquest. Frequently cited for its renowned porcelain production, especially from the official kilns at Jingdezhen.

clues.clue: Founded by the Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang) after the collapse of the Mongol Yuan dynasty
examples: name this dynasty of the Hongwu and Yongle Emperors, which overthrew the Mongol Yuan dynasty.
identify this dynasty whose founding Hongwu Emperor came to power after conquering the Yuan Dynasty.

clues.clue: Succeeded in 1644 by the Qing dynasty following the Manchu conquest
examples: name this Chinese dynasty succeeded in the seventeenth century by the Qing dynasty and famous for its innovations in ceramics.
Zheng He used the Mao Kun map on his seven 'treasure voyages' for this Chinese dynasty, which was succeeded by the Qing dynasty.

clues.clue: Renowned for its porcelain, especially from the official kilns at Jingdezhen
examples: name this Chinese dynasty succeeded in the seventeenth century by the Qing dynasty and famous for its innovations in ceramics.
Name this dynasty founded by Zhu Yuanzhang, whose Yongle emperor send Zheng He on a number of voyages throughout the Indian Ocean. It was noted for its porcelain production.

works.name: The Hongwu Emperor and the Founding of the Dynasty
works.description: The dynasty was founded by Zhu Yuanzhang, a leader of the Red Turban Rebellion who defeated the rival warlord Chen Youliang at the Battle of Lake Poyang to gain control of the Yangtze River before taking the throne as the Hongwu Emperor. As founder, he grew increasingly paranoid and autocratic, warning against eunuch influence, executing tens of thousands over an alleged plot by his chancellor, and abolishing the chancellor position outright, while also sending his sons to the frontiers to smooth his young grandson's succession.

clues.clue: Zhu Yuanzhang, a leader of the Red Turban Rebellion, defeated the rival warlord Chen Youliang at the Battle of Lake Poyang (1363) — called 'perhaps the largest naval battle in history' — to gain control of the Yangtze River
examples: In perhaps the largest naval battle in history, the founder of this dynasty defeated Chen Youliang at the Battle of Lake Poyang, gaining control of the Yangtze River.
The founder of this dynasty defeated a navy under the rival leader Chen Youliang at the Battle of Lake Poyang.

clues.clue: After an alleged plot by chancellor Hu Weiyong to overthrow him, an early emperor ordered tens of thousands of executions and abolished the position of chancellor entirely
examples: An emperor of this dynasty responded to an alleged plot by chancellor Hu Weiyong to overthrow him by ordering tens of thousands of executions and abolishing the position of chancellor.

clues.clue: The founder wrote a tablet warning against the use of eunuchs after coming to power
examples: The founder of this dynasty wrote a tablet warning against using eunuchs after coming to power after the Battle of Lake Poyang.

clues.clue: The dynasty's 'Four Great Trials' included the case of the general Lan Yu, tried for plotting to overthrow the founding Hongwu Emperor
examples: This dynasty's 'Four Great Trials' include the case of Lan Yu, who was plotting to overthrow its founder, the Hongwu Emperor.

clues.clue: A general won the Battle of Buyur Nor against remaining Mongol forces but failed to capture the khan Togus Temur
examples: A general for this dynasty annihilated enemy forces at the Battle of Buyur Nor but failed to capture Togus Temur.

clues.clue: After the death of his oldest son, the founder sent most of his surviving sons to administer frontier regions, clearing the way for his grandson, the Jianwen Emperor, to succeed him
examples: After the death of his oldest son, this dynasty's founder sent most of his sons to administer frontier regions to clear the way for his grandson, the Jianwen Emperor, to succeed him.

works.name: The Yongle Emperor and Zheng He's Treasure Voyages
works.description: The founder's son seized the throne from his nephew Jianwen via the Jingnan Campaign and reigned as the Yongle Emperor, the dynasty's third ruler. He moved the capital from Nanjing to Beijing, built the Forbidden City and the Temple of Heaven, commissioned a massive encyclopedia, and sponsored the eunuch admiral Zheng He's treasure-fleet voyages to the Indian Ocean, which brought back exotica like a giraffe from Malindi before his successor the Hongxi Emperor ended the expeditions.

clues.clue: After the exile of several brothers, a prince launched the Jingnan Campaign to overthrow his nephew, the Jianwen Emperor
examples: After the exile of several of his brothers, a prince of this dynasty launched the Jingnan Campaign to overthrow Jianwen.

clues.clue: The dynasty's third ruler (Yongle) moved the capital from Nanjing to Beijing and sponsored construction of the Forbidden City
examples: This dynasty's third ruler moved his capital from Nanjing to Beijing and sponsored the construction of the Forbidden City.

clues.clue: The Yongle Emperor sponsored the eunuch admiral Zheng He's massive treasure-fleet voyages to the Indian Ocean
examples: That emperor from this dynasty also sponsored massive treasure voyages led by his admiral Zheng He.
name this Chinese dynasty during which Zheng He led several treasure voyages to the Indian ocean.

clues.clue: Zheng He executed the pirate Chen Zuyi and brought back a giraffe from the East African city of Malindi
examples: A eunuch who lived during this dynasty executed the pirate Chen Zuyi and brought a giraffe back from his voyage to Malindi.
During this dynasty, an admiral sent sailors to Mecca and retrieved a giraffe from Malindi.

clues.clue: The Hongxi Emperor ended the treasure-voyage expeditions that Yongle had first chartered
examples: This dynasty's Hongxi emperor ended a series of expeditions that brought a giraffe back from Malindi, which were first chartered by this dynasty's Yongle emperor.

clues.clue: Zheng He navigated using the Mao Kun strip map on his seven treasure voyages
examples: Zheng He used the Mao Kun map on his seven 'treasure voyages' for this Chinese dynasty, which was succeeded by the Qing dynasty.

clues.clue: The Yongle Emperor commissioned a massive encyclopedia; the rebel Li Zicheng may have torched the original copy while retreating from Beijing during the dynasty's fall
examples: A massive encyclopedia was commissioned by this Chinese dynasty's emperor Yongle. Li Zicheng, who overthrew this dynasty and laid the groundwork for the rise of the Qing, may have torched the original copy when retreating from Beijing.
Other works of literature written during this dynasty include Pingyao Zhuan, Romance of the Three Kingdoms and the Yongle Encyclopedia.

clues.clue: The Yongle Emperor also constructed the Temple of Heaven in Beijing
examples: The Temple of Heaven was constructed by this dynasty's Yongle Emperor.

works.name: Eunuch Power and Court Intrigue
works.description: Court eunuchs repeatedly seized outsized power during the dynasty, drawing condemnation from officials like the philosopher Huang Zongxi. The 'Eight Tigers,' led by Liu Jin, dominated the Zhengde Emperor's court, while the eunuch Wei Zhongxian — considered the most powerful eunuch in Chinese history — later crushed the reformist Donglin Academy faction by exploiting an illiterate, carpentry-obsessed emperor, working alongside the concubine Madame Ke and the Embroidered Uniform Guard secret police.

clues.clue: The philosopher Huang Zongxi decried the influence of eunuchs on the dynasty's government, writing that 'eunuchs are like poison and wild beasts'
examples: The philosopher Huang Zongxi decried the influence of eunuchs on this dynasty's government, noting that 'Everyone has known for thousands of years that eunuchs are like poison and wild beasts.'

clues.clue: The 'Eight Tigers,' a group of eunuchs led by Liu Jin, dominated the court of the Zhengde Emperor
examples: Liu Jin led the Eight Tigers during this dynasty.
The 'Eight Tigers' was a group of eunuchs in this dynasty that was led by Liu Jin and dominated the court of the Zhengde Emperor.

clues.clue: Madame Ke rose to power alongside her associate Wei Zhongxian, who was targeted by the dynasty's Embroidered Uniform Guard secret police
examples: During this dynasty, Madame Ke rose to power with her associate Wei Zhongxian, who was targeted by this dynasty's Embroidered Uniform Guard secret police.

clues.clue: The eunuch Wei Zhongxian, considered the most powerful eunuch in Chinese history, dominated the reign of the Tianqi Emperor and crushed the reformist Donglin Academy faction, exploiting the illiterate emperor's obsession with carpentry to gain power
examples: The reign of the Tianqi Emperor during this dynasty was dominated by a man who crushed the reformist Donglin faction and is considered the most powerful eunuch in Chinese history, Wei Zhongxian.

The Donglin Academy faction protested alleged moral decline in this dynasty until brutally suppressed by the eunuch Wei Zhongxian, who took advantage of an illiterate emperor from this dynasty's obsession with carpentry to gain power.

clues.clue: A deranged intruder named Zhang Chai beat a crown prince with a stick after infiltrating the palace; that prince lasted only a month as emperor before dying after taking two suspicious red pills
examples: A nutcase named Zhang Chai beat up a crown prince of this dynasty after infiltrating the palace armed only with a stick.
That prince of this dynasty lasted a month as emperor before dropping dead after taking two dubious red pills.

clues.clue: A 'Daoist Emperor's' refusal to be posthumously adopted by his predecessor inflamed the 'Great Rites Controversy'
examples: The refusal of this dynasty's 'Daoist Emperor' to be adopted by his predecessor inflamed the 'Great Rites Controversy.'

clues.clue: The dynasty's final emperor demoted his brother's wet nurse to the Laundry Department, where she was beaten to death
examples: The final emperor of this dynasty demoted his brother's wet nurse to the Laundry Department, where she was beaten and killed.

works.name: The Tumu Crisis (1449)
works.description: The eunuch Wang Zhen's mismanagement of a military campaign against the Oirats led to a humiliating disaster: the Oirat leaders Esen Taishi and Taisun Khan captured the Zhengtong Emperor (also known as Yingzong) at the Tumu Crisis, a rare instance of a reigning Chinese emperor being taken prisoner in the field.

clues.clue: The eunuch Wang Zhen's failed handling of a military campaign against the Oirats led to the capture of the Zhengtong Emperor (Yingzong) by Esen Taishi and Taisun Khan at the Tumu Crisis of 1449
examples: The failures of the eunuch Wang Jen during a military expedition launched by this dynasty led the Oirats to capture this dynasty's Zhengtong Emperor during the Tumu Crisis.
The Tumu Crisis of 1449 targeted Yingzong of this Chinese dynasty, which was founded by Hongwu after the defeat of the Yuan dynasty.

works.name: The Fall of the Ming and Loyalist Resistance
works.description: The dynasty's final decades were wracked by Little Ice Age famines, floods, and plagues that fueled peasant unrest. The rebel Li Zicheng ultimately sacked Beijing, driving the Chongzhen Emperor to suicide, while the general Wu Sangui let Manchu forces through the Great Wall, sealing the dynasty's fate. After the fall, figures like Wu Sangui (later a rebel himself) and the pirate-loyalist Koxinga continued to fight in the dynasty's name from bases like Taiwan's Kingdom of Tungning.

clues.clue: The peasant rebel Li Zicheng sacked the capital, Beijing, leading the Chongzhen Emperor to commit suicide, ending the dynasty
examples: This dynasty fell after the rebel Li Zicheng sacked its capital, which led Emperor Chongzhen to commit suicide.
Both Wu Sangui and Koxinga served this dynasty, which faced a rebellion by Li Zicheng during its collapse in the mid-seventeenth century.

clues.clue: As the dynasty collapsed, the last emperor severed his own daughter's arm in an attempt to deny spoils to the rebel Li Zicheng, known as the 'Dashing Prince'
examples: This dynasty's last emperor severed his daughter's arm in an attempt to deny spoils to a rebel known as the 'Dashing Prince.'

clues.clue: The general Wu Sangui let Manchu forces pass through the Great Wall, betraying the dynasty to the Qing
examples: By letting enemy forces go around the Great Wall, the general Wu Sangui betrayed this dynasty.

clues.clue: Devastating famines caused by the Little Ice Age, along with floods and plagues, enabled opportunists like Li Zicheng and Wu Sangui to seize power
examples: Name this dynasty that suffered a series of devastating famines due to the effects of the Little Ice Age, as well as floods, plagues, and other calamities that enabled opportunists like Li Zicheng and Wu Sangui to seize power.

clues.clue: The dynasty once paid a mercenary army a bounty for every enemy head, after which the mercenaries massacred the dynasty's own subjects to collect the bounty — an incident recounted in Frederick Wakeman's history of the transition to the Qing, The Great Enterprise
examples: This dynasty once paid a mercenary army for each disembodied head, after which the mercenaries proceeded to massacre a bunch of this dynasty's subjects to collect the bounty.
That incident is recounted in Frederick Wakeman's history of the transition of this dynasty to its successor, titled The Great Enterprise.

clues.clue: The pirate-turned-loyalist Koxinga, who like Wu Sangui had served the dynasty, founded the Kingdom of Tungning on Taiwan as a base for Ming restorationists after the fall to the Qing
examples: The Qing were mainly interested in crushing Koxinga because he, and a bunch of other Chinese who fled to Taiwan, supported the restoration of this dynasty which preceded the Qing and is known for its ceramics.

works.name: Porcelain and Material Culture
works.description: The dynasty's kilns at Jingdezhen produced some of the era's most enduring artifacts: painted duocai and wucai porcelain, the 'secret decoration' an hua technique, and hallmark blue-and-white wares whose reign marks were first applied under the Xuande Emperor, alongside imported cloisonne enamelware. This production was closely tied to global trade, as American silver flowed into the dynasty in exchange for its porcelain and other goods.

clues.clue: The large workshops at Jingdezhen developed porcelain in the duocai and wucai painted styles
examples: The large Jingdezhen factory developed porcelain in the duocai and wucai styles during this dynasty, which lasted from 1368 until 1644.

clues.clue: The an hua ('secret decoration') porcelain technique was first developed at centers like Jingdezhen
examples: It saw the first development of the an hua technique at such places as Jingdezhen.

clues.clue: Cloisonne enamelware, which had spread east from the Byzantines, was used under the Xuande Emperor; reign marks were first applied to cloisonne wares and to hallmark blue-and-white porcelain
examples: Cloisonne spread east from the Byzantines and was used under this dynasty's Xuande Emperor. Reign marks were first applied to cloisonne wares and hallmark blue-and-white porcelain during this Chinese dynasty.

clues.clue: Most silver mined in the Americas ultimately went to purchase this dynasty's goods, including porcelain from the official kilns at Jingdezhen
examples: Most silver mined in the Americas ultimately went to purchase goods from this dynasty, such as from its official kilns at Jingdezhen.

works.name: Literature, Technology, and Foreign Relations
works.description: The dynasty produced some of China's classic vernacular novels, including Journey to the West, Romance of the Three Kingdoms, Water Margin, Investiture of the Gods, and The Peony Pavilion. Militarily and economically, it fielded gunpowder weapons detailed in the Huolongjing, faced chronic wokou pirate raids that its weak navy struggled to contain, and maintained a tributary relationship with neighboring Joseon Korea, while episodes like the salt-labor exemption and the pseudohistorian Gavin Menzies's claims round out its economic and popular legacy.

clues.clue: Classic novels including Journey to the West (Wu Cheng'en), Romance of the Three Kingdoms, Water Margin, Investiture of the Gods, and The Peony Pavilion were all written during this dynasty
examples: Though written in a later dynasty, The Scholars is set during this Chinese dynasty. Journey to the West and Romance of the Three Kingdoms were written during the rule of this dynasty.
Investiture of the Gods was written during the rule of this Chinese Dynasty. It was when Wu Cheng'en wrote Journey to the West.

clues.clue: The military treatise Jixiao xinshu describes a multi-tipped spear called the 'wolf brush,' created for use in the 'mandarin duck formation'
examples: The Jixiao xinshu describes a multi-tipped spear called a 'wolf brush,' created for use in a 'mandarin duck formation' during this dynasty.

clues.clue: The gunpowder weapon called the 'divine crow,' a paper-plane-shaped device, is detailed in the military manual Huolongjing; muskets were later introduced but proved inadequate to stop the Manchu Qing conquest
examples: While it is not considered a gunpowder empire, this dynasty found many different uses for it, like the paper-plane-like 'divine crow' as detailed in the Huolongjing. The introduction of muskets was inadequate to prevent this dynasty from being conquered by the Manchu Qing Dynasty.

clues.clue: The physician Li Shizhen's materia medica Bencao Gangmu, written during this dynasty, described the honey-mummification practice of 'mellification' as an 'Arabic custom'
examples: Mellification of corpses was first described as an 'Arabic custom' in Li Shizhen's Bencao Gangmu, a Chinese materia medica written during this dynasty.

clues.clue: The dynasty's disinterest in maintaining its navy allowed a surge in wokou ('dwarf bandit') pirate activity along the coast; it was the first dynasty to face mass wokou raids and sent Zheng He to suppress pirates in Southeast Asia
examples: This dynasty's disinterest in its navy allowed for a surge in activity among the wokou.
This Chinese dynasty was the first one that had to deal with mass wokou raids. Its other anti-pirate activities include sending Zheng He to crush pirates in Southeast Asia during his voyages.

clues.clue: The dynasty's invasion of Yunnan gave rise to the Tunbao ethnic group
examples: The Tunbao ethnic group arose from this dynasty's invasion of Yunnan.

clues.clue: Households that produced salt were exempted from additional corvee labor
examples: Households producing salt were exempt from additional labor under this dynasty, which overthrew the Mongolian Yuan dynasty in 1368.

clues.clue: Hyperinflation continued after the Yuan's collapse until the dynasty gradually replaced a new 'precious note' currency with silver
examples: Following the Yuan's collapse, hyperinflation continued under this dynasty, which gradually replaced a new 'precious note' with silver.

clues.clue: Neighboring Joseon Korea served as a tributary under the sadae foreign-vassalage policy; King Injo reportedly killed his own son out of loyalty to this dynasty, and Gwanghae was deposed for 'betraying' it
examples: Another work of 'palace literature' covers Queen Inmok's confinement under Gwanghae, who was deposed for 'betraying' this state. King Injo supposedly killed his son out of loyalty to this state, which was the subject of the sadae foreign policy.

clues.clue: Pseudohistorian Gavin Menzies controversially claimed that this dynasty 'discovered the world' via Zheng He's voyages
examples: This dynasty is postulated to have 'discovered the world' by the pseudohistorian Gavin Menzies.

comprehensive_summary: The Ming dynasty (1368-1644) was founded by Zhu Yuanzhang, a leader of the Red Turban Rebellion who defeated the rival warlord Chen Youliang at the Battle of Lake Poyang — called perhaps the largest naval battle in history — to gain control of the Yangtze River before taking the throne as the Hongwu Emperor and driving out the Mongol Yuan dynasty. As founder, Hongwu grew increasingly autocratic: he wrote a tablet warning against eunuch influence, then contradicted its spirit by executing tens of thousands over an alleged plot by his chancellor Hu Weiyong and abolishing the chancellor position outright, prosecuted the general Lan Yu in one of the dynasty's 'Four Great Trials,' and sent his surviving sons to the frontiers after his eldest son's death to smooth the succession of his grandson, the Jianwen Emperor. That succession was soon overturned: after the exile of several brothers, a prince launched the Jingnan Campaign against Jianwen and seized the throne as the Yongle Emperor, the dynasty's third ruler, who moved the capital from Nanjing to Beijing, built the Forbidden City and the Temple of Heaven, commissioned a massive encyclopedia (later partly destroyed as Li Zicheng retreated from Beijing during the dynasty's fall), and sponsored the eunuch admiral Zheng He's treasure-fleet voyages across the Indian Ocean — voyages that executed the pirate Chen Zuyi and brought back a giraffe from the East African city of Malindi, using the Mao Kun strip map, before Yongle's successor, the Hongxi Emperor, ended the expeditions. Court eunuchs repeatedly seized outsized power in the centuries that followed, drawing condemnation from the philosopher Huang Zongxi: the 'Eight Tigers,' led by Liu Jin, dominated the Zhengde Emperor's court, while the eunuch Wei Zhongxian — considered the most powerful eunuch in Chinese history — later crushed the reformist Donglin Academy faction by exploiting an illiterate, carpentry-obsessed emperor, working alongside the concubine Madame Ke and the Embroidered Uniform Guard secret police; other court dramas included a deranged intruder beating a crown prince who died a month into his reign after taking two suspicious red pills, a 'Daoist Emperor' whose refusal of posthumous adoption inflamed the Great Rites Controversy, and a final emperor who had his brother's wet nurse beaten to death. Militarily, the eunuch Wang Zhen's mismanaged campaign against the Oirats led to the Tumu Crisis of 1449, in which the Oirat leaders Esen Taishi and Taisun Khan captured the reigning Zhengtong Emperor (Yingzong) in the field. The dynasty's final decades were wracked by Little Ice Age famines, floods, and plagues that fueled peasant unrest; the rebel Li Zicheng ultimately sacked Beijing, driving the Chongzhen Emperor — who severed his own daughter's arm to deny her to the 'Dashing Prince' Li Zicheng — to suicide, while the general Wu Sangui let Manchu forces through the Great Wall, sealing the dynasty's fate (a fate darkly echoed by an earlier episode in which mercenaries paid a bounty per enemy head instead massacred the dynasty's own subjects, recounted in Frederick Wakeman's The Great Enterprise). After the fall, the pirate-turned-loyalist Koxinga, who like Wu Sangui had served the dynasty, founded the Kingdom of Tungning on Taiwan as a base for Ming restorationists. Beyond its political history, the dynasty is renowned for the porcelain of its Jingdezhen kilns — painted duocai and wucai wares, the 'secret decoration' an hua technique, and hallmark blue-and-white wares whose reign marks were first applied alongside imported cloisonne enamelware under the Xuande Emperor — production closely tied to global trade, as American silver flowed in to purchase Ming goods. Culturally, it produced classic vernacular novels including Journey to the West, Romance of the Three Kingdoms, Water Margin, Investiture of the Gods, and The Peony Pavilion, alongside military and scientific texts like the Jixiao xinshu (describing the 'wolf brush' spear and 'mandarin duck formation'), the gunpowder manual Huolongjing (detailing the 'divine crow' device, though muskets proved inadequate against the Manchu Qing), and Li Shizhen's materia medica Bencao Gangmu. The dynasty's disinterest in its navy allowed chronic wokou pirate raids along the coast — it was the first dynasty to face them at scale — while its invasion of Yunnan gave rise to the Tunbao ethnic group, salt-producing households were exempted from labor, hyperinflation from the Yuan era was gradually tamed by a shift to silver currency, and neighboring Joseon Korea served as a tributary under the sadae policy. The pseudohistorian Gavin Menzies later controversially claimed the dynasty 'discovered the world' via Zheng He's voyages.
recursive_suggestions: Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang) — founder with substantial independent clue material (Battle of Lake Poyang, Hu Weiyong purge, Four Great Trials); could merit his own topic with more independent answerline presence
Yongle Emperor — recurring figure (Jingnan Campaign, Forbidden City, Zheng He patronage, Yongle Encyclopedia) with strong independent presence
Zheng He — recurring figure with substantial independent material (treasure voyages, Malindi giraffe, Mao Kun map); watch for overlap with this page's voyages section
Wei Zhongxian — recurring eunuch figure (Donglin Academy suppression, Tianqi Emperor) with some independent presence
Tumu Crisis — has independent narrative material (Wang Zhen, Esen Taishi, Zhengtong Emperor) and could become its own topic if it recurs as a standalone answerline
Li Zicheng — recurring rebel figure tied to the dynasty's fall; independent answerline presence in some sets
Wu Sangui — recurring figure spanning the fall of the Ming and the later Revolt of the Three Feudatories under the Qing; category filter to History
Koxinga (Zheng Chenggong) — recurring figure with independent material (Kingdom of Tungning, Siege of Fort Zeelandia); category filter to History
Jingdezhen / Ming porcelain — recurring decorative-arts topic; category filter to Fine Arts (Visual Fine Arts) to avoid overlap with this page's brief treatment
